data.title: Pathward Financial raises 2026 EPS guidance to $8.55-$9.05 while expanding partner pipeline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pathward Financial, Inc. (CASH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Brett Pharr stated that "we kicked off the year in a position of strength" and emphasized Pathward's unique approach to banking, highlighting its focus on payment facilitation through issuing sponsorship, merchant acquiring sponsorship, and digital payments. Pharr noted, "Because of our differences, we are able to generate significant fee income." He outlined the company's four principal pillars: experienced leadership in payments, streamlined operating structure, partnership commitment, and strong governance in risk and compliance.
  • Pharr announced the rollout of an evolved operating model designed to align with partners and enable client success, stating, "We believe this decision ultimately positions our clients for greater success and revenue enablement and the company for increased innovation and growth."
  • Pharr signaled that revenue growth for the fiscal year will come from optimizing the balance sheet, increasing fee income from balance sheet velocity, and expanding noninterest income through new contracts, such as merchant acquiring sponsorships.
  • The company reported a strong start to tax season, with "over 11% more enrolled tax offices than at this point last year," renewed agreements with tax software partners, and continued technology improvements for efficiency.
  • CFO Gregory Sigrist reported, "We were pleased with our results in the quarter, which were marked by solid growth in our core business, growing interest income and commercial finance with a lower provision, increasing core card and deposit fee income and flat expenses."

Outlook

  • Pathward increased its fiscal year 2026 guidance to an EPS range of $8.55 to $9.05, with assumptions of no additional rate cuts, an effective tax rate of 18% to 22%, and expected share repurchases.
  • Pharr noted, "We feel confident that we will be able to deliver on our goals and look forward to providing a more robust tax update next quarter."
  • Management emphasized that revenue growth is prioritized in areas not dependent on balance sheet expansion, with a focus on balance sheet optimization and partner-driven fee income.

Financial Results

  • Net income for the quarter was $35.2 million and earnings per diluted share were $1.57, representing increases of 17% and 28%, respectively, compared to last year.
  • Return on average assets was reported as 1.87% and return on average tangible equity was 26.7% for the quarter.
  • Deposits held at December 31 totaled $6.4 billion, a $170 million decrease versus a year ago, mainly due to changes in custodial deposit balances.
  • Loans and leases at December 31 were $5 billion, with a $531 million increase in commercial finance loans and a $148 million decrease in consumer finance loans.
  • The company originated $1.9 billion in loans during the quarter, including $678 million in commercial finance and $1.2 billion in consumer finance.
  • Noninterest expenses were slightly lower than last year, with lower rate-related card processing fees attributed to a lower rate environment.
  • Liquidity remains strong with $3.7 billion available.
  • The company repurchased approximately 652,000 shares at an average price of $72.07, leaving 4.3 million shares available under the current program.

Q&A

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW, asked about the net interest margin (NIM) trajectory. Gregory Sigrist responded that, stripping out the impact from gross HFI consumer loans, "adjusted NIM of 5.11% a year ago. Last quarter would have been 5.31%, and this quarter would have been 5.49%." Sigrist added, "I think that 5.31% is the launch point for the second quarter. And again, as long as the macroeconomic environment stays muted and middle part of the curve is elevated. We still have some tailwinds."
  • Switzer inquired about fintechs obtaining bank charters and the potential threat to Pathward’s business. CEO Pharr said, "It's going to be a long time before it creates any competitive pressure."
  • Switzer asked about Pathward's involvement in digital assets. Pharr explained, "We're already doing some things in the crypto space in terms of onboarding and offboarding the fiat currency, et cetera. To be clear, we don't hold any of those digital assets today."
  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James, questioned the growth potential from new partner cohorts. Sigrist said, "once each of those programs is launched and live, I would expect contribution to a full 12-month run rate on the card fee line to be somewhere in the middle to high single digits, and that's just based on those programs."
  • Yanchunis asked about the partner pipeline and the outlook for new announcements. Pharr responded, "The pipeline has never been more full."
  • On updated guidance, Yanchunis queried about the factors determining the upper or lower end. Pharr and Sigrist pointed to partner ramp timing and the success of the tax season as the main swing factors.
  • Yanchunis asked about new loan originations and held-for-sale balances. Sigrist replied, "I'm optimistic that as the existing partners ramp and scale that, that volume is going to continue to certainly on a year-over-year basis, scale versus where it was."
  • Yanchunis inquired about credit portfolio differences versus three months ago. Pharr answered, "There's nothing in there systematic."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive and probing tone, focusing on NIM trends, partner growth, guidance, and the competitive landscape. There was repeated interest in the sustainability of growth and partner pipeline.
  • Management’s tone was confident and optimistic, especially in prepared remarks and when discussing partner momentum and guidance. Phrases like "we are comfortable with our credit trends" and "we're really enthusiastic" reflected high confidence. Responses to questions about competitive threats and digital assets were measured and reassuring.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to more pronounced confidence, especially in guidance and partner pipeline commentary. Analyst sentiment remained constructive and focused on fundamental growth drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 EPS was raised from $8.25-$8.75 to $8.55-$9.05, reflecting greater confidence in revenue growth from new partners and fee income.
  • Management’s strategic focus is now more explicitly on optimizing the balance sheet through velocity and generating fee income, rather than balance sheet expansion.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from clarifying new contracts and product launches last quarter to drilling into the pace and sustainability of growth from partner programs and core financial metrics.
  • Key metrics such as EPS, net income, and loan originations saw significant increases versus the prior year quarter, with management emphasizing improved efficiency and partner ramp-up.
  • Management’s confidence in technology investments and partner pipeline increased, while analysts maintained a focus on monitoring credit trends and the impact of industry shifts.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Sigrist highlighted that nonperforming loans "ticked up slightly when compared to last quarter" but stated these are "in different verticals, and we do not believe represent a systemic portfolio issue."
  • Management reiterated its focus on annualized net charge-offs rather than nonperforming loan volumes, supported by a collateralized lending approach.
  • The company cited potential timing impacts from the government shutdown on secondary market revenues but expects a rebound.
  • Tax season performance and partner ramp timing were noted as key variables affecting future results.

Final Takeaway

Pathward Financial entered the new fiscal year with strengthened performance, a robust and expanding partner pipeline, and a raised earnings outlook for 2026. Management expressed confidence in the company’s differentiated business model, growth from new and existing partners, and disciplined risk management, indicating that these elements position Pathward to achieve its financial goals and drive innovation and efficiency in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Associated Banc-Corp outlines 9–10% C&I loan growth for 2026 while expanding in Omaha, Kansas City, and Dallas
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Associated Banc-Corp (ASB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Andrew Harmening emphasized that 2025 was a pivotal year, marking the completion of all major Phase 2 strategic plan investments and delivering "our strongest year for organic household growth since we began tracking a decade ago with net growth in all four quarters of 2025." He highlighted over $1.2 billion in relationship C&I loan growth and nearly $1 billion in core customer deposit growth for the year. Harmening stated, "Over each of the last three quarters, we set a company record for net interest income. We also saw strength in several fee income categories in the back half of the year."
  • The company announced an agreement to acquire American National Corporation, enabling entry into the Omaha market and strengthening its Twin Cities presence. Harmening noted, "We believe that Associated and American National are a natural cultural fit, and we look forward to welcoming American National employees and customers to Associated later this year."
  • Associated Banc-Corp is planning several additional investments in 2026 to accelerate growth in the Twin Cities, Omaha, Kansas City, and Dallas. The CEO said, "We’re planning to increase acquisition-focused marketing spend in the Twin Cities and Omaha by over 100% between the two markets combined. The total marketing acquisition spend across all markets will increase by 25%."
  • CFO Derek Meyer reported, "Q4 net interest income of $310 million set another record for the strongest quarterly NII in company history, and our NII was up 15% for the year." Meyer further highlighted a "net interest margin increased 2 basis points to 3.06% for the quarter."
  • Chief Credit Officer Patrick Ahern stated, "In Q4, our criticized loans decreased, and our nonaccruals dipped to 32 basis points of total loans. Net charge-offs decreased to just 3 basis points for the quarter and 12 basis points for the full year."

Outlook

  • Associated Banc-Corp expects C&I loan growth of 9% to 10% in 2026. Harmening explained, "We expect C&I loan growth of 9% to 10% in 2026. At the top of the house, we expect total bank loan growth of 5% to 6% for the year. Both growth figures are on a stand-alone basis, excluding the impact of American National."
  • The company projects core customer deposit growth of 5% to 6% for 2026, excluding American National.
  • Net interest income growth is forecasted between 5.5% and 6.5% for 2026, assuming two Fed rate cuts and excluding the American National acquisition.
  • Noninterest income is expected to grow by 4% to 5% in 2026.
  • Total noninterest expense growth is projected at 3% in 2026, also excluding the acquisition impact.

Financial Results

  • Reported Q4 earnings of $0.80 per share and $2.77 per share for the full year.
  • Total loans grew by 1% versus the prior quarter and 5% versus Q4 2024.
  • Core customer deposits increased nearly $700 million compared to Q3 and about $1 billion compared to Q4 of the previous year.
  • Investment security balances reached $9.3 billion in Q4, and the securities plus cash to total assets ratio climbed to 24.3%.
  • The allowance for credit losses increased by $5 million to $419 million, with the ACL ratio up 1 basis point to 1.35%.
  • Tangible book value per share rose to above $22, a $0.65 increase over Q3.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, CGS International, asked about the impact of the American National acquisition on net interest income. Harmening responded that "we don't have a lot of updates on that on the financial end of that. Obviously, we're going through the approval process, and we're hopeful to close in the second quarter and integrate in the third quarter.”
  • Tamayo inquired about the expense growth tied to investments in four cities. Harmening stated, "We've given the expense target -- expense growth target at 3%. And just as in every other year, we've made some hard decisions on where to cut costs to invest."
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler, questioned the sustainability of C&I growth and the runoff in residential real estate. Harmening replied, "We think the resi will continue to run off at a pretty similar pace," and noted that this would allow margin expansion as low-margin loans are replaced with higher-margin ones.
  • Siefers also asked about capital markets fee levels. Meyer answered, "I feel very strong and it's repeatable. Yes, I think the point, Scott, you were right last quarter, we were thrilled at how strong capital markets was in our fees overall."
  • Terence McEvoy, Stephens, asked about margin expansion and deposit betas. Meyer responded, "You are correct, it implies some expansion. We've been in all of our forecast scenarios, I've been saying this for a few quarters, we typically see our NIM trickling up."
  • Andrew Leischner, KBW, raised potential for buybacks. Harmening clarified, "Our #1 priority is organic growth. We're in the middle of a deal and our main priority of that is execution and with that then organic growth."
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets, discussed deposit mix and growth. Harmening indicated, "We're focusing on getting to 2% [household growth]. And that will have -- that will over time, have meaningful growth in what our deposit mix is in a positive way."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was positive regarding loan growth and strategic expansion, but remained inquisitive on acquisition impacts, fee sustainability, and expense controls.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, particularly regarding organic growth, expansion into new metropolitan markets, and repeatability of capital markets fee income. Harmening used phrases like "we're excited about the future of this company" and "it gives us a bit of confidence heading into the year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts showed increased confidence, with more forward-looking growth targets and detailed plans for new market penetration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to higher specificity, with explicit 9–10% C&I loan growth and 5–6% deposit growth targets, compared to more general growth expectations in Q3.
  • Strategic focus has intensified on major metropolitan market expansion, especially with the American National acquisition and investments in Dallas, Kansas City, and the Twin Cities.
  • Analysts concentrated more on acquisition impacts and the sustainability of recent strong results, as opposed to the Q3 focus on organic growth and margin resilience.
  • Key financial metrics showed improvement: net interest income, earnings per share, core deposit growth, and tangible book value per share all increased quarter-over-quarter.
  • Management’s confidence and willingness to provide detailed market-level plans and hiring intentions increased from Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management continues to monitor macroeconomic conditions, including inflation, labor markets, tariffs, and elevated interest rates.
  • Ahern cited ongoing vigilance in reviewing portfolios and staying in contact with customers to stay ahead of emerging risks.
  • The company is watching the effects of elevated interest rates on portfolios and maintains ongoing interest rate sensitivity analysis bank-wide.
  • Analysts raised questions about potential expense increases from new market investments and the sustainability of capital markets fee income.

Final Takeaway

Associated Banc-Corp’s management reiterated its commitment to organic growth and disciplined expansion, projecting significant C&I loan and deposit growth in 2026 while investing in key metropolitan markets and integrating the American National acquisition. The company’s record-setting performance in 2025, strong balance sheet remix, and clear forward guidance reflect sustained momentum and a focus on profitability and risk management for the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alaska Air points to a pickup in bookings late in Q4
data.description:
Alaska Air Group (ALK) reported revenue was $3.63 billion for its fourth quarter to match the consensus expectation. Revenue per available seat mile was up 0.6% year-over-year despite the airline contending with a temporary demand pullback from the government shutdown in November. Corporate travel grew 9% year-over-year during the quarter, while close-in demand remained strong throughout the quarter as bookings and yields continued to rebound from the challenging environment earlier in the year. 

The airline company highlighted that premium revenue increased 7% year-over-year, cargo revenue was up 22%, and loyalty revenue increased 12%. Notably, commercial initiatives and synergy capture remained on track for the company for the fourth consecutive quarter. The government shutdown and an IT outage dropped the profit line during the quarter.

Looking ahead, Alaska Air (ALK) said its guidance for 2026 reflects a wide range of potential macroeconomic outcomes. "We expect to continue to realize value from Alaska Accelerate initiatives and synergies from the Hawaiian integration, which remain on track or ahead of plan relative to our initial expectations. To hit the higher end of our guidance range, we would require sustained macroeconomic recovery in 2026, at or improving on trends seen in the first three weeks of the year, and for fuel prices to stabilize," updated the company.

For the full year, ALK  sees EPS of $3.50 to $6.50 (midpoint $5.00) vs. $5.52 consensus. Capacity is expected to be 2% to 3% higher.

Shares of Alaska Air (ALK) dipped 0.9% in postmarket trading.

data.title: Suncor oil worker death company's first since 2022 CEO departure
data.description:
Provincial authorities in Alberta said Thursday they are investigating last week's death of a worker at Suncor Energy's (SU) Fort Hills oil sands site near Fort McMurray, the company's first on-site fatality since a former CEO stepped down in 2022 following a series of deadly incidents.

Suncor (SU) said the worker was operating a piece of heavy equipment on January 13 when it sank into the muskeg, a type of watery bog; the person has still not been found, and the company said the incident may have resulted from a medical event.

The fatality marks the first at a Suncor (SU) site since 2022, when a spate of incidents prompted calls for a major shakeup of the company, with activist Elliott Investment Management leading the campaign that resulted in then-CEO Mark Little stepping down; he was replaced by former Imperial Oil head Rich Kruger, who remains in charge at Suncor.

data.title: Old Republic signals continued specialty growth and pricing discipline into 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Old Republic International Corporation (ORI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Craig Smiddy, President, CEO & Director, stated that "in the fourth quarter, we produced $236 million of consolidated pretax operating income compared to $285 million, and our consolidated combined ratio was 96% compared to 92.7%." He highlighted a full year pretax operating income of $1 billion and a consolidated combined ratio of 94.7%. Smiddy pointed out growth in book value per share, including dividends, of 22%, attributing it to "strong operating earnings, our higher investment valuations and our sound capital management strategy."

  • Specialty Insurance grew net premiums earned by 8.3% in the quarter and 10.9% for the year, exceeding $5 billion for the first time. Specialty produced $178 million of pretax operating income for the quarter and $900 million for the year, with a combined ratio for the year at 93.2%. Title grew premium and fees by 12.4% in the quarter and 9.1% for the year, with $65 million of pretax operating income in Q4 and a combined ratio of 94%.

  • Francis Sodaro, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "This morning, we reported net operating income of $185 million for the quarter compared to $227 million last year. On a per share basis, comparable quarter-over-quarter results were $0.74 compared to $0.90." He noted a 7.9% increase in net investment income for the quarter and capital return initiatives of over $1 billion for the year, with $850 million remaining in the repurchase program.

  • Carolyn Monroe, President, reported, "Title reported premium and fee revenue for the quarter of $789 million. This represents an increase of 12% from fourth quarter of last year." She emphasized commercial premiums rising to 29% of earned premiums, investment income up nearly 12%, and expense management resulting in a 1.2% decrease in operating expenses relative to premium and fees.

Outlook

  • Smiddy described plans for 2026: "ending the year at a combined ratio in Specialty of 93.2% feels pretty good to us. We're hopefully, over time, be a bit better than that. But I think where we're at in the P&C cycle, that feels pretty good. And relative to next year, our plan is to produce something around the same level." He added, "I would say 2026, we're looking for a very consistent year."

  • Monroe noted, "commercial should have about a 15% to 20% improvement over this year, which was a wonderful year for commercial. But they see a single-digit increase in residential for next year, less than 10%. So... another year like this year, with some improvement, especially if commercial does improve like it did."

Financial Results

  • Net operating income for the quarter was $185 million, with $0.74 per share. Net investment income rose 7.9% for the quarter. Book value per share ended at $24.21, marking a 22% increase for the year. Nearly $700 million in dividends were declared, and $56 million in share repurchases occurred during the quarter. Total capital return for the year exceeded $1 billion.

  • Specialty Insurance net premiums written increased 6.1% in the quarter. New Specialty operating companies contributed over $300 million in net premiums written in 2025 and delivered positive operating income.

  • Commercial auto net premiums written grew 6.4% in the quarter, with the loss ratio at 80%. Rate increases accelerated to 16% for commercial auto. Workers' comp net premiums written was 6% lower, with a loss ratio of 65.2%. Property net premiums written increased 21% in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Asked about Specialty Insurance combined ratio targets for 2026 and drivers of higher loss picks. Craig Smiddy responded, "ending the year at a combined ratio in Specialty of 93.2% feels pretty good to us... our plan is to produce something around the same level." On loss picks, Smiddy explained, "as we got towards the end of the year, while we did not notice any paid claim difference, we did notice that case reserves were higher... trends now look to be rather than in the low teens, the mid-teens."

  • Peters: Inquired about capital position post-dividend. Smiddy replied, "we still think we have plenty of capital. So we think that will put us in a position to, again, recommend to the Board a regular dividend increase... we also have $850 million still remaining on our share repurchase program."

  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler: Asked about patterns in case reserves and commercial auto trends. Smiddy explained, "the number of people making bodily injury claims... is higher. And the percentage of bodily injury claims with attorney representation is higher... our view is that litigation system abuse is accounting for these increases."

  • Newsome: Questioned risk management in commercial auto. Smiddy noted, "our most sophisticated data and analytics area is within our long-haul trucking company... helps us with targeting rate to those customers that elevate higher propensity for loss."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was inquisitive but mildly cautious, with repeated questions about reserving practices, loss trends, and capital management.

  • Management's tone was confident and steady in prepared remarks, but more defensive and detailed in Q&A, especially in discussing loss trends and reserving, as seen in Smiddy's comments: "it's impossible to instantaneously know what your severity is... the best anyone can do is make a projection based on current observations."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst focus remained on capital deployment and reserving. Management's confidence was consistent but more explicit about rapid response to loss trend changes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to a focus on maintaining combined ratios at current levels, with less emphasis on expansion. Strategic focus highlighted ongoing investment in Specialty operating companies and technology.

  • Analysts continued to focus on capital management and reserving discipline. Key metric changes included higher rate increases in commercial auto and a sharper rise in property premium growth.

  • Management's confidence remained steady, but there was increased discussion of rising loss trends, especially in commercial auto, and a stronger emphasis on rate discipline and risk selection.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited higher case reserves, increased bodily injury claims, greater attorney involvement, and litigation system abuse as ongoing risks in commercial auto.

  • Smiddy explained, "trends now look to be rather than in the low teens, the mid-teens... litigation system abuse is accounting for these increases."

  • Analysts highlighted concerns about reserving adequacy and the impact of capital returns on future flexibility.

Final Takeaway

Old Republic closed 2025 with record operating income and continued robust capital returns, driven by growth in Specialty and Title segments and supported by disciplined underwriting and rate management. Management aims to maintain profitability and combined ratios into 2026, while closely monitoring loss trends—particularly in commercial auto—and maintaining flexibility for capital deployment through dividends and share repurchases. The company remains focused on underwriting discipline and investment in technology and new Specialty companies to support long-term growth and resilience in a shifting market environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Shell considers exit from Argentina's Vaca Muerta shale play - Reuters
data.description:
Shell (SHEL) is considering a sale of its assets in Argentina's Vaca Muerta shale play and has approached potential buyers in recent weeks to gauge their interest, Reuters reported Thursday.

Shell (SHEL) is said to be open to selling some or all of its interests in the Vaca Muerta, and the company's assets likely would be valued in the billions of dollars, although a precise estimate is difficult because some of the assets are undeveloped, the report said.

The company entered Vaca Muerta in 2012, and a full sale would mark a surprise exit by one of the play's earliest backers, just as interest in the region is growing due to concerns that other large shale fields have peaked.

Shale pioneer Harold Hamm's Continental Resources recently acquired minority stakes in four Vaca Muerta blocks from Pan American Energy, calling the region "one of the most compelling shale plays in the world."

Only ~8% of the Vaca Muerta is under development, and the play is estimated to hold the world's second-largest shale gas and fourth-largest shale oil resources, according to U.S. government statistics.

data.title: Intuitive Surgical beats on both lines on da Vinci growth
data.description:
• Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) is up ~2% in after-hours trading Thursday after posting Q4 results that beat on both lines driven by strong year-over-year growth in placements of its da Vinci surgical systems.
  • In the quarter ended Dec. 31, the company placed 532 units compared to 493 in the year-ago period.
  • Intuitive was helped in the quarter by a 19% YoY increase in revenue to $2.87B.
  • Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $2.53 compares to $2.21 in Q4 2024.
  • Intuitive ended 2025 with cash, cash equivalents, and investments of ~$9B, compared to $8.8B at the end of 2024. 
  • In 2026, the company sees da Vinci procedure growth of ~13%-15% compared to 18% in 2025.

data.title: Aris Mining streamlines board and management, appoints founder as CEO
data.description:
• Ian Telfer retired as chair and director of Aris Mining (ARIS:CA) but will remain an advisor.
  • Founder Neil Woodyer was appointed chair and chief executive officer.
  • David Garofalo was named lead independent director to strengthen independent board oversight.
  • Doug Bowlby was promoted to president.
  • The chief operating officer role was eliminated, and Richard Thomas has departed the company.

data.title: Gold and silver rack up new record highs on geopolitical tensions, softer U.S. dollar
data.description:
Gold futures closed above $4,900/oz for the first time Thursday, and silver also surged to a fresh all-time high, sparked by ‌continued geopolitical tensions, a softer U.S. dollar and expectations of rate cuts from the U.S. Federal Reserve.

The gains came even after President Trump said a "framework of a future deal" for U.S. access to Greenland was reached and that new tariffs against European Union countries would not be implemented, as the details of any agreement were unclear.

The latest report on U.S. Personal Consumption Expenditures showed rising consumer spending in November and October, indicating a third straight quarter of strong growth.

Goldman Sachs raised its year-end gold price forecast to $5,400/oz from $4,900/oz previously, citing a broadening of private sector diversification into bullion.

"We assume that private sector diversification buyers - whose purchases hedge global policy risks and have driven the upside surprise to our price forecast - don't liquidate their gold holdings in 2026, effectively lifting the starting point of our price forecast," Goldman wrote, adding that it expects central bank purchases to average 60 tons in 2026 as emerging market central banks continue diversifying reserves into gold, contributing to the price increase.

Silver also is continuing its scorching rally, establishing a new record high above $95/oz, and has a "far more compelling fundamental narrative than gold," Tradu senior analyst Nikos Tzabouras said in a note. "Maybe it's not a reserve asset in the way that gold is, but it still benefits from safe-haven flows, and ​dollar weakness."

Although the silver market remains well supported over the long term, BCA Research chief strategist Roukaya Ibrahim warned a speculative frenzy has become the main driving force behind the metal's rally and could prove unsustainable.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for January delivery closed +1.6% to $4,908.80/oz, and front-month January Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) settled +4.1% to $95.976/oz, both new record highs.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)
